evidence_quotes.quote: publishers and studio's are able to take away my products at any moments notice.
Nowhere was there a clear sign that I was essentially paying for a license, not a product.
but they should not be able to remove my access to the product I have paid for.

paragraphs.text: As someone who entertains himself with gaming, I see many positive feedback on this form. But I do think an additional point is missing. As it stands now in the gaming entertainment industry, publishers and studio's are able to take away my products at any moments notice. Products I have paid for. Nowhere was there a clear sign that I was essentially paying for a license, not a product. This is highly unfair, no matter how you look at it. This is mostly seen with so called "Live service games", Games that are fully online and meant to be played online together with others. Now do I expect a company to provide service to a product that is not financial stable, for example when the game itself is old and maybe new versions have been published. But that does not mean someone can take away my product I have payed money for. There are many other viable ways to use that product, even if the support for the product has stopped. For example, there are many games that does not receive official support anymore, but are continued by a fan base driven group that provide the servers to play on. So to make it clear: I do not expect any company to give eternal support to a product, but they should not be able to remove my access to the product I have paid for. I think this is a highly unfair business practice.
